13 min read + 22 min video
We’re going to start this module with an overview of the Soap
Opera Series (SOS).
Soap Opera Series (SOS)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 22 --

Closed captioning (subtitles) will be available for this video in the
next two days.
The Soap Opera Series (Details)
A Soap Opera Series (SOS) is a serialized, cohesive collection of
emails based on a theme, idea, or story that serves as a vehicle for
people to experience and journey deeper into our world.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 22 --

Important characteristics of an SOS include:
▪ Each SOS has a beginning and an end.
▪ An SOS can last for days or weeks (longer in some rare
examples).
▪ Email frequency can be adjusted to match your needs and
the needs of your audience (for example, ten emails in a row
delivered daily, seven emails sent every two days, etc.).
▪ Uses narrative-driven themes and arcs as the emotional
delivery vehicle for your marketing message. The e�ect, from
a subscriber’s point of view, is a feeling of being emotionally
pulled forward, wanting to know more, bonding with you,
and moving closer to your worldview.
▪ Creates a frame and establishes context that your audience
needs to understand and internalize in order to �gure out
where you �t in their world, and how the value you have to
offer relates to them speci�cally (or, when there is
incongruence, or values don’t align, doesn’t).
▪ In general, an SOS does not sell. However, an SOS can be a
vehicle that sets up and ampli�es future sales.
A Soap Opera Series uses principles from serialized narrative to
pull audiences forward using tension/contrast, themes/narrative
arcs, implications, open loops, and cliffhangers.
Each email is an installment in a larger narrative journey, and
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 22 --

those installments are designed to pull the reader’s attention
forward continuously toward a predetermined destination that
you know but your audience does not.
Along the way there are twists and turns, sub-plots within the
larger plot, hurdles to overcome, goals to be achieved, insights to
be discovered, and the implied promise of transformation for the
audience.
And all of this is framed within a journey that is exciting,
entertaining, and informative for your audience. A feeling
encapsulated in one word: experiential.
The best way to understand a Soap Opera Series is to think about
your favorite episodic TV show. Each individual show �ts within a
larger narrative structure (usually a season), and most shows
eventually include several seasons.
Like this:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 22 --

… or this:
An SOS can be triggered anytime and anywhere, you get to put it
in places that matter to how you’re building your world, like this:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 22 --

Viewers’ attention is pulled forward scene by scene through
individual email episodes, and they’re always left wanting more
(which pulls their attention from one email episode to the next).
Seasons often end with dramatic cliffhangers, which leaves the
audience anticipating the upcoming season with burning, edge-
of-their seat desire.
Throughout each episode and each season, tension is increased,
then resolved, then further increased, and resolved, in a never-
ending series of scenes and transitions that capture their hearts,
imagination, attention, and move them emotionally.
As our dear friend Michael Hauge says (and repeatedly tells us): “…
the primary goal of a story (or any storyteller) is to elicit emotion.”
“Your goal is, and always must be, to elicit emotion in your readers and
audiences. And the primary source of emotion in any story is con�ict.
It’s the obstacles your hero must face that create the empathy that is
critical to your story’s success.” — Michael Hauge
A Soap Opera Series should give your audience the same
experience as serialized, episodic TV by using the same story
principles.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 22 --

Let’s explore the process in detail.
What Is The Journey? (De�ning A to
B)
The �rst consideration for creating a powerful Soap Opera Series
is establishing a clear understanding of the journey you want
your audience to experience.
Where are they now, and where do you want them to be at the end
of your SOS?
For example, an SOS-powered journey can take a prospect from:
▪ Uninformed to informed about a topic you know well.
▪ Misinformed to informed about a topic because of some
unique insight (or ‘secret sauce’) you’ve developed.
▪ Feeling hopeless about a problem to hopeful about your
potential solution to that problem.
▪ Confused about a topic or opportunity to understanding that
topic or opportunity.
▪ Overwhelmed by options to focused on a critical few choices
that really matter.
▪ Etc.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 22 --

The speci�cs will be unique to your experience, expertise, values,
audience needs and desires, and the world you’re building for
your prospects and customers to inhabit.
The critical ingredient in each of these examples is contrast
between two points; A and B, where A is your prospect when s/he
enters your world, and B is where you want your prospect to be
when s/he completes your Soap Opera Series.
We’ve described contrast in detail in Module 2.
Once we know the A and B of our Soap Opera Series, we need to
identify a ‘vehicle’ and a ‘container’ for that journey.
Vehicles include stories, themes, or interesting ideas that establish
the narrative arc for the entire journey.
Containers are the underlying structure of the Soap Opera Series.
Examples include mini-courses, serialized, �rst-person stories,
case study-style narratives, expert explanations, etc.
Together, vehicles and containers connects the dots between A
and B for our audience, and help us organize our thinking and
writing so that it’s cohesive and effective.
For example, you might tell your story from being uninformed to
informed in some topic in an SOS, highlighting the bumps in the
road along the way. Showing an audience what you’ve discovered
that works, and saving them time by avoiding what doesn’t work,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 22 --

is an effective option for a story-based SOS.
Another alternative, popularized by Donald Miller, is to establish
your audience as the hero of the story, speaking to their external
and internal needs and desires, while positioning you as a
trustworthy, credible guide with a plan to help your audience
achieve what they want.
Fictional characters, like Frank & Matt, can represent audience
archetypes, or you can tell real stories about real people that
represent the A to B journey your audience is experiencing
through your SOS.
There’s no speci�c ‘right way’ to do this. What matters most is
that the journey of transformation from A to B is easily
understood by your audience. It helps (dramatically) if your
audience can see themselves in the character(s) you’re using in
your writing, and those connections can be made explicitly and
implicitly.
Theme is another way to establish a narrative arc for a Soap Opera
Series.
The easiest way to think about theme is by answering the
question: “From the 10,000′ view, what’s this SOS all about?”
Don’t worry if the theme doesn’t rise to the level of literary
greatness. What matters is it’s clear, guides your writing, and
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 22 --

in�uences your prospect’s journey from A to B.
We recommend that you read and study Module 2 before writing
your �rst Soap Opera Series.
Helpful Exercise (Discovering the A
to B of an SOS)
When you create an SOS, we highly recommend that you draw
the series on a sheet of paper (analog — pencil, pen, paper). On the
left is the starting point — this is where a prospect �rst
encounters your world.
Put a dot on your paper there to represent that and label it E1.
On the far right is the end point. That’s where the SOS ends. Put a
dot there and don’t worry about labeling it yet (unless you’re 100%
certain about how many emails you’ll create in your SOS).
Connect those two points with a line or an arc — whatever feels
right to you.
Then add some notes about who your prospect is when s/he
receives that �rst email. What does s/he want to avoid or achieve?
What functional, social, and/or emotional needs is s/he trying to
ful�ll?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 22 --

Label that “A” on your drawing.
Then, on the right, add notes about who your prospect has
become by the end of your SOS. What does s/he know and
understand now that s/he didn’t before?
What has s/he experienced as a result of the world you’ve built
with your SOS?
Label that “B” on your drawing.
Next, draw the dots on the line or arc to represent each of the
emails you want to include in your SOS, and label those E1, E2, E3,
etc. There’s no right answer for how many emails you should
include. Instead, ask yourself how many you need to take your
audience on a journey from A to B effectively?
For your �rst SOS, we recommend more than three emails and
fewer than ten. It’s hard work to pull an audience’s attention
forward day after day — set yourself up for success with an SOS
around 5-9 emails initially, and adjust (or not) over time as you
become more experienced.
Once you have this drawing, brainstorm ideas for connecting A to
B. Is there a story you want to tell (about yourself, a client, a
customer, or a �ctional archetype)?
Or is there a theme — like Courage & Perseverance — that you
could use to frame the journey?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 22 --

Don’t try to �nd the perfect idea — instead, focus on identifying a
lot of ideas.
Quotes, books, interviews, movie scenes, life events, stories, and
much more can serve as inspiration. Get the ideas out of your
head an on to the paper, and don’t try to edit along the way.
Once you have created this document, pay very close attention to
your energy levels. You’ll likely �nd that your attention begins to
wane sometime during idea development.
That’s perfectly natural, and it’s part of the cycle of creativity
captured in The Breakout Principle.
Struggle Release Breakout Improved New Normal
Generating ideas is, by de�nition, a struggle. Two to three hours
of idea generation is a lot of work and the next step is to release
that work and move on to something else.
(Preferably not something cognitively demanding.)
If your experience is anything like ours — and The Breakout
Principle suggests it will be — ideas for your SOS’s narrative arc
will begin to emerge 12-48 hours after your �rst brainstorming
session. That’s the ‘breakout’ experience described in The
Breakout Principle. (And a very accurate description of our
experience of the Muse.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 22 --

(We stack the deck in our favor by writing Morning Pages too,
and that’s a practice we highly recommend to our students as
well.)
Storyboard Method
You may �nd that a storyboard is helpful to externalize ideas, see
the �ow of emails in an SOS, draw attention to loops that are
opened and closed, identify placement for strategic cliffhangers,
etc.
Here are a couple of examples.
Blank Storyboard Example
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 22 --

Example Storyboard for Our Emergent
Marketing Series
Note: The �rst image (left) is the web page with opt-in. Then E0
(immediate) through E4 in one view.
You don’t have to use storyboards, but it’s worth your time to
experiment with them to see if they’re valuable for your
work�ow. There’s something magical about seeing the emails in
one view spread across the screen.
Writing your SOS
Once you have the main idea / narrative arc for your Soap Opera
Series, and you’ve identi�ed some (or all) of the ingredients, it’s
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 22 --

time to start writing.
Broadly speaking, there are two ways to approach this writing.
One is more deliberate, and the other is more emergent.
Neither is right or wrong, better or worse. Each of us approaches
writing (very) differently — André is an emergent (discovery)
writer, who often writes to �nd and express his ideas to be
organized and polished in later rounds of editing.
When André writes an SOS, he starts with a “Draft Zero” which is
free writing to get thoughts out of his head and onto the page.
As ideas coalesce, André writes an Anne Lamott-inspired “shitty
�rst draft,” which inspires additional rounds of increasingly
focused, organized, polished writing.
Sub-plots, stories within stories, relevant tangents, etc. emerge for
André along the way as part of the writing process.
Shawn, on the other hand, is a deliberate writer. In the spirit of
John McPhee, he spends a lot of time before writing to identify and
organize his ideas. He approaches writing like a prosecutor
making a case.
Instead of free writing a “Draft Zero,” Shawn creates a
compilation of ideas in a single overview document, organizes
those ideas into a coherent structure, and then painstakingly
crafts a lead to ‘hang’ the rest of his writing on.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 22 --

Shawn also edits as he writes, continually re�ning paragraphs
and sentences until they ‘feel’ right to him (which usually means
that they’re razor sharp and ‘�ow’ logically from one point to the
next).
Again, neither approach is right or wrong, in general, but each is
exactly right for us individually.
We can’t tell you what approach to writing will be write for you,
but we encourage you to pay attention to signals and re�ne your
process along the way.
If you need structure, start with a loose outline. If you �nd
structure demotivating, let ‘er rip and start writing.
Pay attention to what works for you, and then continue to hone
what works and discard what doesn’t. There is no ‘right’ way to
write, there’s just an evolution over time of what brings our your
best.
If you’re not sure where to start, try free writing (as explained by
Mark Levy here, author of the brilliant book, Accidental Genius).
Put your butt in a chair and write conversationally, like you’re
emailing a friend, and keep going until you reach a stopping
point.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 22 --

That might be one terrible email or one perfect paragraph.
Whatever it is, just write. Think about the narrative arc you’ve
identi�ed, where your prospect is on day one, and then start
writing.
Then take a break and do it again.
Repeat that process until your SOS is �nished. Seriously. Your
�rst version won’t be perfect, and it shouldn’t be. Perfect is the
enemy of �nished. (This is a lesson we know all too well.)
No one’s written more episodes of The Simpsons than John
Swartzwelder (59 in total). His secret was simple, expressed
beautifully in a rare interview he did with The New Yorker in May
2021.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 22 --

Here are a few tips to make your SOS more interesting and
engaging for your audience.
Tip #1 — match your tone to your audience’s expectations. In
general, we recommend that you write conversationally, like
you’re emailing a friend.
However, if your audience is very technical, or has other, similar
expectations, then it’s important that your writing voice/tone
re�ects those nuances too.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 22 --

There’s no prescriptive answer for how to do this because every
situation is unique. A general guiding principle is to write the way
you would talk to an ideal prospect.
(Pro tip: read your emails — and other writing — aloud during the
editing process. You’ll hear subtleties in your writing that you
won’t always see on the screen.)
Now that we understand what an SOS is, and how to write one,
let’s discuss what what we’re trying to accomplish with a Soap
Opera Series and why they’re so incredibly important.
An SOS is the foundation of the ‘trust engine’ we’re building with
an audience. (The Relationship Building Series and Value
Newsletter also are components of the AoE trust engine, which
we discuss in the following modules.)
An SOS does three things exceptionally well.
First, an SOS helps you establish an empathic bond with your
audience. It says to your audience “this is who I am,” “this is what
I care about,” and “this is how I choose to show up in the world.”
Second, an SOS demonstrates credibility, authority, and
(eventually) preeminence. It creates opportunities to say “this is
my experience,” “this is who I’ve helped and how I’ve helped
them,” and “this is how I can add meaningful value to your life.”
Third, an SOS frames everything that follows.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 22 --

It establishes the lens through which you audience sees the world
you’re showing them. That lens is explicit, through the content
your share, and implicit, by the values you demonstrate.
A signi�cant part of that implicit framing is that you’re not trying
to sell anything with an SOS. At �rst that may seem counter-
intuitive, but once you understand how all of the parts of the AoE
trust and revenue engines �t together, you’ll see why this decision
is so powerful.
Up next we’ll describe the Relationship Building Series (a speci�c
type of Soap Opera Series with a few unique characteristics). If
this is your �rst time through the Art of Email, we encourage you
to review all of the modules from beginning to end before doing
any of the work.
If this is your second (or more) time through, we encourage you to
focus your energy and attention on creating your �rst
Relationship Building Series. It is instrumental in the success of
everything that follows.
Questions or feedback — please use the comments below.
Action Items (Lesson Summary)
If this is your �rst time through the Art of Email, feel free to move
on to the next module before completing these action items.
▪ Identify the journey you want to take your audience on.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 22 --

Where are they now (A), and where do you want them to be
at the end of your SOS (B)?
▪ Identify the ‘vehicle’ and ‘container’ for your SOS.
▪ Externalize and capture the ingredients to include in your
SOS (quotes, stories, characters, links to supporting
resources, etc.).
▪ Create a draft map of your SOS with pen/pencil and paper.
Label A (beginning) and B (end), and identify with a dot the
individual emails for the entire SOS.
▪ Add your ideas for the SOS where you think they �t (loosely)
in the progression from A to B.
▪ Create a digital storyboard of your SOS with one document
per email, arranged in order (left to right).
▪ Start �lling in details from your analog drawing into the
digital storyboard. Add nuances like open loops and
cliffhangers where appropriate.
▪ Write a version of your Soap Opera Series. This will likely
take several writing sessions. Emphasize speed and quality
(in that order). You’re trying to write an “80% quality” draft,
meaning it’s good but not perfect.
▪ Keep writing your �rst SOS until it’s complete. Do not move
on to other parts of the AoE email system until you have an
SOS that you’re able to share with an audience.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
